title: Lokta paper sheet forming
subtitle: Pulling sheets by hand using lokta fibre in the Nepalese tradition
excerpt: Lokta paper is made from the bark fibres of Daphne papyracea, a shrub native to the Himalayan foothills. It is among the world's most durable hand-formed papers: the long interlocking fibres resist tearing, hold a sharp pen line, and age without brittleness. This tutorial forms small sheets using pre-prepared dry lokta fibre strips.
sourceNotes: Tim Barrett, Japanese Papermaking: Traditions, Tools, and Techniques (1983, Weatherhill), adapted for lokta fibre technique. Also draws on Winifred Lutz workshop notes from the Paper and Book Intensive (1995). The floating-vat technique described here follows standard Asian papermaking practice.

content.text: Lokta paper has a look of its own. The edges sit a little uneven from the mould's deckle frame, and you can see long
running across the sheet face. It pulls differently from cotton-rag paper. The long fibres sit less evenly in the
than cotton linters do, so the mould needs a firmer shake to spread them out.

items.name: Dried lokta fibre strips (or pre-processed lokta fibre sheets, torn to small pieces)

items.qty: 1, filled to 10 cm with warm water
items.name: Papermaking vat (larger than the A4 mould + deckle)

items.qty: 1, never use a kitchen blender that also processes food
items.name: Dedicated blender for pulp only
Clean felts or a smooth board for couching onto
Heavy boards and weights for pressing

attrs.heading: Materials for one A4 session (6-8 sheets)

content.text: Soak dried lokta fibre strips in warm water for 30 minutes. Then tear into small pieces (roughly 5 cm squares) and blend a small handful at a time in the papermaking blender with 500 ml water for 30-45 seconds. Unlike cotton linters, lokta fibre does not break down completely in the blender: some longer fibres remain. This is correct, they give the sheet its characteristic long-fibre texture. Blend in batches and pour the pulp into the vat.

Add water to the vat until the slurry looks almost clear when you hold a handful to the light. Stir thoroughly. Hold the mould and deckle together (deckle on top, mesh face up), and lower them into the vat at a 45° angle, scooping forward under the surface in a single smooth pass. Lift the mould straight up and level it immediately. Shake the mould gently fore-and-aft and side-to-side for five seconds to even the fibre distribution, then hold still and let the water drain.
Once the mould stops dripping (about 30 seconds), remove the deckle.

the sheet by turning the mould face-down onto a damp felt or clean board in one decisive movement. Press the mould back firmly, then peel it up from one corner, the wet sheet should release cleanly onto the felt. Blot the surface with the papermaking sponge and press under boards and weight for 20 minutes, then peel and dry flat on a clean board. Lokta dries faster than cotton and shrinks slightly at the edges, let it dry completely before trimming.
